EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure continued moving west into Arizona by the end of July while moisture was recharged across the state behind a strong back door cold front. North to south steering flow with abundant shear and instability led to the development of numerous showers and storms during the early to mid afternoon hours. Several of these storms were strong to severe as they progressed southward across the eastern plains and through the Rio Grande Valley. A storm near Las Vegas dumped hail up to the size of golf balls with very heavy rainfall and strong winds. Another storm moved south across Interstate 25 near San Jose and produced quarter size hail. Storms that developed over the Jemez Mountains moved south through Cochiti Pueblo with quarter size hail before blasting through the Albuquerque metro area with damaging winds and heavy rainfall. Ruidoso also experienced severe weather with quarter size hail and heavy rainfall. Minor flooding was reported in many areas as well. Swift runoff into the urban drainage system in Albuquerque washed two individuals down the Embudo Arroyo. One individual was rescued however the other died in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail with a severe thunderstorm as it tracked south through Cochiti Pueblo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very dry month with little in the way of monsoon moisture worsened drought conditions across southwest and south-central New Mexico. Some areas around the Gila region were placed into Exceptional(D4) drought conditions while most other areas were at least in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Extreme(D3) drought conditions for the month of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely deep upper low was moving into the Four Corners region while a back door cold front moved into the region bringing record low temperatures to the Borderland.  Southwest flow aloft combined with the cold front to bring severe thunderstorms on the afternoon and early evening of the 8th while prolonged lift over the stalled front around Luna county brought flooding rains during the first half of the day on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the High Rolls CWOP station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door frontal boundary that brought higher moisture levels and scattered thunderstorms to northeast New Mexico on July 11, 2020 became stationary over the area through July 12, 2020. The combination of strong afternoon heating with abundant moisture and instability led to the development of a few more showers and thunderstorms over Union and Harding counties. A couple of these storms produced strong winds, hail, and heavy rainfall. Nickel size hail was reported near Capulin while another storm produced severe wind gusts of 70 mph near Mills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported near Capulin.

The combination of strong afternoon heating with abundant moisture and instability led to the development of a few more showers and thunderstorms over Union and Harding counties. A couple of these storms produced strong winds, hail, and heavy rainfall. Nickel size hail was reported near Capulin while another storm produced severe wind gusts of 70 mph near Mills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds up to 70 mph were reported near Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture continued deepening over New Mexico as southerly flow increased across the state on July 17, 2020. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed along and east of the central mountain chain during the late afternoon hours before shifting north and east into the nearby highlands. One particularly heavy thunderstorm approached the Interstate 40 corridor near Clines Corners and produced a brief wind gust up to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported a brief thunderstorm outflow wind gust up to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure moved west into Arizona and allowed drier air to filter into central and western parts of the state on July 29, 2020. Just enough moisture was in place with stronger northwest flow aloft to allow a couple storms to develop along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. One storm moved off the high terrain and produced a brief wind gust up to 58 mph at Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raton airport reported a peak wind gust up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist back door front entered northeast New Mexico during the early morning hours of July 11, 2020. The combination of strong daytime heating and abundant moisture with northwest flow aloft led to the development of isolated showers and thunderstorms across the northeast plains. Several of these storms became strong with gusty winds, hail, heavy rainfall, and frequent lightning. Hail up to the size of quarters was reported with a storm that became severe over eastern San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell near Bell Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions in June 2020 improved to moderate to severe drought across most of Union County in July thanks to above normal precipitation. Northern portions of the county remained in extreme drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Tome-Adelino recorded a 59 mph wind gust as a severe thunderstorm continued to track south through the Rio Grande Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep tap of monsoon moisture continued surging north into New Mexico on July 24, 2020, setting the stage for additional showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall, localized flash flooding, and strong microburst winds. Storms developed early in the afternoon then moved slowly north to northwest across most of New Mexico. Several of these thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall amounts over two inches along with small hail and strong microburst winds. The Clayton airport reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph and the Roswell airport reported a peak wind gust up to 68 mph. Straight-line winds damaged power lines, power poles, roofs, fences, and trees in the area around Midway. Heavy rainfall around Alamo damaged culverts and roadways in the area. A flash flood near Lemitar washed a pickup truck into an arroyo and caused damage to roadways. Fortunately, no injuries occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was reported at the Clayton airport.

Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from June 2020 improved to severe drought in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep tap of monsoon moisture continued surging north into New Mexico on July 24, 2020, setting the stage for additional showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall, localized flash flooding, and strong microburst winds. Storms developed early in the afternoon then moved slowly north to northwest across most of New Mexico. Several of these thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall amounts over two inches along with small hail and strong microburst winds. The Clayton airport reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph and the Roswell airport reported a peak wind gust up to 68 mph. Straight-line winds damaged power lines, power poles, roofs, fences, and trees in the area around Midway. Heavy rainfall around Alamo damaged culverts and roadways in the area. A flash flood near Lemitar washed a pickup truck into an arroyo and caused damage to roadways. Fortunately, no injuries occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding damaged several roadways and culverts around Alamo. Emergency management estimated damages between $60,000 and $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from June 2020 worsened in July with all of the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains in extreme drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was centered over the Desert Southwest with southeast New Mexico on the eastern edge of this ridge. A surface boundary from thunderstorms the previous night was pushed up against the mountains in southeast New Mexico. Moist and unstable air was in place across the area along with sufficient wind shear. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that developed in southeast New Mexico with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a 61 mph wind gust near Whites City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough over the Western U.S. brought strong winds to the Guadalupe Mountains.

A moisture-laden storm system over the eastern Pacific shifted slowly northeast into New Mexico on January 16, 2020. Meanwhile, a shallow back door cold front pushed southwest into the eastern plains with temperatures just below freezing. The combination of a deep plume of moisture with shallow cold air resulted in light to moderate freezing rain over the east central and northeast plains on the 16th. Many areas picked up between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch of ice, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Temperatures were just cold enough for heavy, wet snow to fall over northern and western New Mexico, including the Rio Grande Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from six to 12 inches over the high terrain with amounts of two to six inches around Albuquerque, Santa Fe, and Taos. Impacts were limited to elevated roadways and higher terrain passes. Strong west winds then developed in the wake of this system as it shifted in the Great Plains. Wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph were common over the northern high terrain, the east slopes of the central mountain chain, and parts of northeaster New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 52 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and northern New Mexico at the end of December 2019 continued in January 2020. While severe drought conditions improved over the northern mountains of New Mexico, abnormally dry to moderate drought conditions developed across east-central NM. Moderate to severe drought conditions worsened to about 32 percent of the state of which 12 percent across northwestern and northeastern NM was deemed as severe drought. January 2020 saw an active pattern impact much of NM, and the northern mountains continued to build an above normal snowpack. However, only light to moderate precipitation amounts in lower elevations resulted in a month that was drier than normal. Precipitation amounts across western New Mexico during January ranged from one half inch to one inch. Central NM as well as the northwest corner of the state generally received half an inch of precipitation while areas across the northern high terrain received amounts near to above an inch. Eastern NM received the least amount of precipitation with amounts generally less than half an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from December 2019 continued in January 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving quickly east through the central Rockies combined with a deepening surface low over eastern New Mexico to produce an area of strong winds over the eastern plains on January 13, 2020. Many areas from the central mountain chain eastward experienced peak wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. The strongest winds impacted the area south and east of Corona where sustained speeds around 45 mph were reported for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CSBF Transwestern Pump station reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 47 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving across the desert southwest and into West Texas brought strong winds to the Guadalupe Mountains.

A more traditional tap of monsoon moisture surged north into New Mexico on July 22, 2020, setting the stage for showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall. The strongest thunderstorms impacted the area from Socorro County eastward through Lincoln County where recent heavy rainfall helped increase the risk for flash flooding. Heavy rainfall produced minor flooding around Magdalena where a couple arroyos neared bank full shortly before 5 pm. Another round of heavy rainfall over the Gallinas Mountains topped arroyo banks and led to flash flooding west of U.S. Highway 54. A section of county road A-019 was partially washed out as flood waters raged through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding partially washed out a section of CR-A019 four miles west of U.S. Highway 54. Damage amounts are estimated for repairs to the county road and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture continued to stream into New Mexico which along with daytime heating, helped to generate another round of thunderstorms across the area on July 14, 2020. Thunderstorms initially formed early in the afternoon over the western and northern high terrain before moving eastward into valley locations. With relatively drier low-levels, gusty outflow winds continued to be the main hazard with one storm producing a severe thunderstorm wind gust near Dunken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph was recorded near Dunken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure system responsible for several consecutive days of record high temperatures and dry conditions finally began to weaken and shift eastward toward the Texas Panhandle. This allowed mid-level moisture to stream into portions of western and central NM which resulted in scattered thunderstorms developing during the afternoon and evening hours on July 13, 2020. Storms initially formed over the western and northern high terrain early in the afternoon before moving eastward into valley locations. With drier low-levels, the main hazards with these storms was strong outflow winds. One severe storm in the Albuquerque metro area produced winds near 60 mph which reduced visibility and downed a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds ahead of a severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 58 mph at the Albuquerque Sunport. These strong winds produced blowing dust which reduced visibility to one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As monsoonal moisture continued to stream into New Mexico, a backdoor cold front that moved through eastern NM helped set the stage for severe weather on July 15, 2020. With increasing low-level moisture, instability, and shear focused along this boundary, the storms that moved off the central mountain chain interacted with the boundary and intensified. Through the afternoon and evening hours, the storms grew upscale into a broken line that moved northeast through the eastern plains. Strong thunderstorm winds were reported ahead of these storms while large hail was reported near Capulin. A brief gustnado was also observed in rural Socorro County along a strong outflow boundary moving through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters near Capulin reported hail up to ping pong size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system to the west of New Mexico on March 26, 2020 spread powerful jet stream winds over the state through the 27th. A strengthening surface low pressure center along the Front Range of the Rockies with deep mixing and warm temperatures on the 26th allowed strong southwest winds to develop in many areas. Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common across eastern New Mexico. The core of the upper level storm system moved into the Four Corners region on the 27th and forced a potent cold front through the state through the evening hours. Southwest to west winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph impacted much of central and eastern New Mexico. Blowing dust from northern Chihuahua spread northeast across south central and southweast New Mexico. Strong west to northwest winds then impacted much of central and eastern New Mexico through early on the 28th in the wake of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sierra Blanca regional airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 52 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from June 2020 continued in July.

Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common across eastern New Mexico. The core of the upper level storm system moved into the Four Corners region on the 27th and forced a potent cold front through the state through the evening hours. Southwest to west winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph impacted much of central and eastern New Mexico. Blowing dust from northern Chihuahua spread northeast across south central and southweast New Mexico. Strong west to northwest winds then impacted much of central and eastern New Mexico through early on the 28th in the wake of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari airport reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and northern New Mexico at the end of February 2020 continued in March. Moderate to severe drought conditions improved to about 26 percent of the state of which 12 percent across northwestern and northeastern NM was deemed as severe drought. The southern half of the state was generally favored for near to above normal precipitation throughout the month which helped improve abnormally dry and moderate drought conditions at the end of February. However, areas of the state where drought conditions are the worst continued to receive below normal precipitation. Precipitation amounts across western New Mexico during March generally ranged from one half to one inch with locally higher amounts across the higher terrain. Central NM generally received one half to three quarters of an inch of precipitation while areas across the northern high terrain received amounts near to above an inch. Due to a couple storm systems that tracked through the area, eastern NM received the most precipitation with totals around one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from February 2020 continued in March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of June. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 53 percent of the state of which about 11 percent across northern NM was deemed as extreme drought. Very dry conditions continued through the first half of June with a more active pattern presenting itself at the end of the month. Despite the more active pattern, storm coverage and precipitation totals were not enough to improve drought conditions especially across the north with the extreme drought conditions. Precipitation totals during the month of June ranged from 1 to 2 inches across central and eastern NM with less than an inch across north central and northeast NM. The Four Corners area of the state continued to receive the least amount of precipitation with rain totals less than half an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from May 2020 continued in June. Conditions deteriorated to extreme drought in eastern Harding County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from June 2020 continued in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture streaming into eastern New Mexico from the Gulf Coast and northern Mexico interacted with a weak shortwave trough approaching from the west to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms over the region on July 4, 2020. Showers and storms first developed along the central mountain chain then spread southeast into the eastern plains where very heavy rainfall, strong wind gusts, hail, and frequent lightning impacted the region. A series of slow-moving storms developed over the Pine Lodge burn scar in Lincoln County and dumped an estimated one to two inches of rain over the area. Flash flooding was observed along NM-246 between mile markers 30 and 31 with up to 12 inches of water, mud, and debris over the roadway across a 30 yard stretch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 12 inches of water, mud, and debris flowed over NM-246 between mile markers 30 and 31. NMDOT was dispatched to clean up the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deeper moisture continued spreading into New Mexico as the center of upper level high pressure over the Four Corners region weakened further on July 19, 2020. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the mid to late afternoon hours along the central mountain chain before drifting southeast into nearby highlands and plains. Several of these thunderstorms produced a quick one to two inches of rain with minor flooding observed around Pojoaque, Santa Fe, Logan, and the Caprock region. The strongest thunderstorms generated torrential rainfall rates over two inches per hour with localized flash flooding in the high terrain west of Ruidoso and west of Corona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hiking trails and a road were washed out west of Bonito Lake due to heavy rainfall over the Little Bear burn scar from 2012. Hikers and campers in the area were evacuated. Fortunately, there were no injuries. Damage amounts are estimated for repairs to the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall caused flash flooding of arroyos along the southeast slope of the Gallinas Mountains west of Corona. A ranch owner observed trees falling into a nearby arroyo as the embankment gave way to flood waters. At least two cattle tank dams were also being over topped in the area. Damage amounts are estimated to repair infrastructure of two cattle tanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was located over the Rio Grande Valley with an unusually moist southeast flow ahead of it that made it into Otero County.  This combined with strong southwest flow aloft including a 120-knot jet led to favorable conditions for a tornado to form across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eye-witness accounts, from two residents located on both ends of|the damage track, provided visual evidence of the occurrence of a|tornado. In addition, a video was taken of tornado formation just|upstream of the damage area. The community impacted is bounded by|open desert to the south and north with little to no access. Thus|the event could have been of somewhat longer duration and track|length, but no evidence was obtainable to support that. The|community is only 0.80 miles wide along the damage track and there|was damage at both ends as the storm entered the community on the|south and exited to the north. Most of the damage was light and|superficial to the homes impacted. At least six homes sustained|damage, mainly associated with their roofs. The damage appeared to|be a combination of tornado and straight-line winds. A toppled|mature pine tree and tossed pergola indicated rotation in the|damage pattern. A resident who witnessed most of the short-lived|event reported seeing a funnel pass over his neighbor`s home and|then touch down in mesquite covered open ground beside his home.|He indicated the circulation was no bigger than his home. The|tornado likely lifted and then touched back down on the north end|of the community where it did its most significant damage to a|home. It removed the homes back porch, lifted a large section of|the roof, and spread the debris in a 120-degree swath downwind.|This homeowner watched the tornado approach and pass from his|workshop, about 50 ft separated from the home. An anemometer at|this home, on a post 4.5 ft off the ground, registered a 61 mph|wind gust at the time of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was moving through the western United States with a 125-knot jet moving across the Borderland.  A surface low deepened over northeastern New Mexico which aided in the strong winds across the region with a peak gust of 88 mph reported at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 61 mph was recorded 18 miles northeast of the White Sands Missile Range main post.  A gust of 60 mph was also recorded 3 miles north-northeast of Capitol Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture surged northwest into eastern New Mexico as an upper level low pressure system approached the southwest United States from the west on March 17, 2020. Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the Caprock region during the early morning then spread northeast throughout the afternoon. A storm that moved through extreme southeast produced a brief tornado near Pleasant Hill, NM shortly after 5 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near Pleasant Hill, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approaching New Mexico from the west interacted with deepening surface low pressure over the eastern plains to produce high winds over parts of eastern New Mexico on March 19, 2020. The strongest winds impacted the area from near Las Vegas northeastward into Union County where several locations reported peak winds of 60 to 70 mph. A high wind gust in Capulin toppled a small carport and a dead elm tree. No injuries were reported with any of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various stations reported peak wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph in the area around Las Vegas, including 64 mph at the Las Vegas airport and 75 mph at a public weather station near Tecolotito. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approaching New Mexico from the west interacted with deepening surface low pressure over the eastern plains to produce high winds over parts of eastern New Mexico on March 19, 2020. A high wind gust in Capulin toppled a small carport and a dead elm tree. No injuries were reported with any of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station at Conchas Dam reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 43 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough to the west of New Mexico with sufficient moisture and instability spreading into the Four Corners area allowed a few showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon of March 21, 2020. One of these thunderstorms produced a brief landspout tornado over the Navajo Agricultural Product Industry farmland complex south of Bloomfield, NM. No damage was observed over rural farmland from this brief storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado touched down over the NAPI farm complex south of Bloomfield, NM. Time and location are estimated from imagery posted to Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest flow aloft over northern New Mexico produced mountain wave activity along the east slopes of the central mountain chain during the late evening hours of March 23, 2020. Most locations experienced wind gusts in the 35 to 45 mph range for several hours. However, a mountain wave crashed at the Raton Airport and produced a peak wind gust up to 61 mph. No damage was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton airport reported a peak wind gust up to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated virga showers and dry thunderstorms developed along the Continental Divide and near the central mountain chain during the afternoon of May 19, 2020. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were common with many of these showers and storms as the airmass was ripe for dry downburst winds. A couple locations reported wind gusts near 60 mph with blowing dust and even some wind damage. Farther east, a sharp dryline stretched along the Texas state line where a couple showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall, hail, and gusty winds during the evening hours. The strongest storms impacted parts of Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A virga shower produced a brief wind gust up to 61 mph at Stanley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of May with extreme drought conditions developing across portions of north central NM. Moderate to extreme drought conditions worsened to about 38 percent of the state of which about 23 percent across northern NM was deemed as severe to extreme drought. Very dry conditions continued through much of the month of May with precipitation totals throughout the area generally less than half an inch. The exception was across far northeastern NM where a more favorable storm track produced precipitation totals near an inch. Poor snowpack across the northern and western high terrain at the end of April continued to deteriorate throughout the month with essentially near-zero percent of normal values by the end of May. Runoff was well below normal on all rivers and tributaries with even some sections of the Rio Grande running dry below Isleta Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from April 2020 continued in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough moving into the southern Rockies on June 7, 2020 allowed a lee side trough to deepen over northeast New Mexico. Southwest winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 45 mph were common across much of central and eastern New Mexico. The strongest winds impacted the eastern plains where peak winds gusted to near 58 mph from near Raton to Capulin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 59 mph were reported near Capulin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton airport reported sustained winds up to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door cold front sagged southwestward into northeast New Mexico on June 18, 2020. The combination of brisk northwest flow aloft with deeper moisture and instability along the cold front boundary set the stage for isolated showers and thunderstorms across the area. One strong thunderstorm developed over Harding County and produced heavy rainfall, gusty winds, and hail up to the size of nickels near Rosebud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels fell a few miles northwest of Rosebud. The combination of hail and strong winds was enough to damage several window screens at a residence. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for two windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold airmass moved into New Mexico in the wake of a fast-moving upper level trough and associated cold front on April 12, 2020. A second upper level low pressure system then surged south through the central Rockies and into western New Mexico early on April 13, 2020. The combination of unseasonably cold air across the state with abundant moisture allowed widespread rain and snow to develop along the I-40 corridor from western New Mexico across the central mountain chain and into portions of eastern New Mexico. Snowfall amounts were heavy for mid April with several locations reporting in excess of eight inches, especially from the Jemez Mountains southeast into the Sandia and Manzano mountains. Difficult travel conditions were reported in many areas, particularly the higher terrain. Strong surface high pressure building into the state behind this system led to some record cold low temperatures and a hard freeze in many areas that had not seen freezing temperatures in several weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches. The Pedernal COOP observer reported a new record daily snowfall of 4.4 inches for April 14th. Difficult driving conditions were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of June. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 53 percent of the state of which about 11 percent across northern NM was deemed as extreme drought. Very dry conditions continued through the first half of June with a more active pattern presenting itself at the end of the month. Despite the more active pattern, storm coverage and precipitation totals were not enough to improve drought conditions especially across the north with the extreme drought conditions. Precipitation totals during the month of June ranged from 1 to 2 inches across central and eastern NM with less than an inch across north central and northeast NM. The Four Corners area of the state continued to receive the least amount of precipitation with rain totals less than half an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from May 2020 continued in June. Drought conditions in eastern San Juan County worsened to extreme drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream approaching New Mexico from the northwest allowed surface low pressure to deepen over the eastern plains on April 23, 2020. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions across portions of western and northern New Mexico at the end of March 2020 worsened in April. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level storm system over Southern California slowly moved eastward over southern New Mexico through February 12, 2020. Precipitation first began across western New Mexico late on the 10th as moisture streamed into the area ahead of this system. Meanwhile, a cold front surged south and west through eastern New Mexico, into the Rio Grande Valley, and to the Arizona border on the morning of the 11th, which cooled temperatures and helped set the stage for widespread wintry precipitation. This cold front was also responsible for strong winds in the Albuquerque metro area which resulted in large variability in snow totals. Snow was moderate to heavy at times, especially across southern New Mexico. Most of the state received snow with generally two to four inches being observed across lower elevations while the highest accumulations were observed across mountain areas with peaks receiving over a foot. Widespread travel impacts were also observed with this system with several accidents reported which caused parts of highways and interstates to be closed. Snow finally ended early in the morning on the 12th as the system lifted northeast into the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandia and Manzano mountains received very heavy snow from this event and various sources reported between 7 and 13 inches. The Sandia ski area reported a storm total of 13 inches. Areas in and around Edgewood also received heavy snow with totals between 7 and 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was lifting out of the Baja region and into southern Arizona.  A 130-knot jet streak was located over southern New Mexico which combined with a back door cold front to produce snow amounts over 7 inches in the lowlands and Gila region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall across the zone ranged from 6.8 inches in Kingston, 4.5 inches 9 miles east of Bayard to 3.7 inches 3 miles north of Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system that developed over the Great Basin on February 2, 2020 moved slowly southeast toward New Mexico through the 4th. Strong southwest winds developed out ahead of this storm system with many areas reporting peak wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph. The strongest winds impacted the southern high terrain where gusts over 60 mph were reported. A powerful cold front then blasted through the region during the evening of 3rd with more strong winds and dramatically colder temperatures. Snow developed in the wake of the cold front across much of northern and central New Mexico including the eastern plains. The combination of very cold temperatures with snow led to high snow ratios and many areas picking up three to six inches. The Sangre de Cristo and Sacramento mountains faired very well with snowfall in excess of one foot. Bitter cold air settled into the region in the wake of this system with widespread single digits and teens across the state. Parts of northern New Mexico fell into the single digits to teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts up to 60 mph were reported along parts of the U.S. 60 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm system that developed over the Great Basin on February 2, 2020 moved slowly southeast toward New Mexico through the 4th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system deepening over the Great Plains forced a powerful back door cold front through eastern New Mexico on February 25, 2020. North winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts near 70 mph focused over Union County. A 75-ft pine tree was toppled onto the Hass Funeral Home resulting in minor damage to the property. This large tree was a landmark for the Clayton area and sources indicated the tree will be used to create two memorial benches for the funeral home. A metal roof was partially blown off a golf cart building at the Clayton Golf Club. A trampoline was missing after it was blown out of a residential back yard. A truck bed liner was found in a street after it had been blown from a pick up truck. Fortunately, no injuries occurred due to these high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 50 mph for a couple hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton airport reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 67 mph for a few hours. An observer at the Holiday Hotel reported a peak wind gust up to 72 mph. Fortunately, no injuries occurred with these high winds. Damage amounts are estimated for minor damage to the roof of the funeral home, damage at the golf course, total loss of one trampoline, and a bed liner that blew out of a pick up truck.

Bitter cold air settled into the region in the wake of this system with widespread single digits and teens across the state. Parts of northern New Mexico fell into the single digits to teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various spotters in the area around Roswell, Hagerman, and Elkins reported 3 to 5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level storm system over Southern California slowly moved eastward over southern New Mexico through February 12, 2020. Precipitation first began across western New Mexico late on the 10th as moisture streamed into the area ahead of this system. Meanwhile, a cold front surged south and west through eastern New Mexico, into the Rio Grande Valley, and to the Arizona border on the morning of the 11th, which cooled temperatures and helped set the stage for widespread wintry precipitation. This cold front was also responsible for strong winds in the Albuquerque metro area which resulted in large variability in snow totals. Snow was moderate to heavy at times, especially across southern New Mexico. Most of the state received snow with generally two to four inches being observed across lower elevations while the highest accumulations were observed across mountain areas with peaks receiving over a foot. Widespread travel impacts were also observed with this system with several accidents reported which caused parts of highways and interstates to be closed. Snow finally ended early in the morning on the 12th as the system lifted northeast into the Texas Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported severe driving conditions along I-40 with very icy roads in the San Fidel area. At least three motor vehicle accidents were reported with two rollovers, however, no information on any damage or injuries was available from law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly moist surface layer was in place with a deep upper low located over the northern Baja region.  A strong 130-knot jet was located over southern New Mexico providing ample shear for some stronger storms to develop.

An upper low was lifting out of the Baja region and into southern Arizona.  A 130-knot jet streak was located over southern New Mexico which combined with a back door cold front to produce snow amounts over 7 inches in the lowlands and Gila region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4.8 and 4.0 inches were reported in Hillsboro.

At the same time a strong arctic cold front moved through West Texas and southeastern New Mexico bringing temperatures well below freezing. Precipitation began on the evening of the 4th that quickly transitioned to snow overnight that continued well into the afternoon of the 5th causing isolated snowfall amounts of almost a foot, with widespread amounts of 3 to 8 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, dry day across the Borderland with high temperatures near 100 over the lowlands and dew points in the mid 40s to lower 50s. A weak mid-level trough was moving through  the region helping to trigger thunderstorms that produced gusty winds due to the dry low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site on the White Sands Missile Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some weak rain showers occurred during the late evening hours over the Gila region and moved toward Truth or Consequences.  A dry low-level environment and the shower moving off the higher elevation brought a wind gust of 70 mph to the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light showers in the area. A few tree limbs were blown down along with other items at the KTCS airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of monsoonal moisture was located across the region with an upper trough over the west coast and an upper high over the southern plains.  A couple of strong thunderstorms fired near the Arizona border in the late morning which produced a brief funnel cloud near Rodeo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public captured a photo of a well-developed funnel cloud north of Rodeo, NM just east of Highway 80.  No debris is seen at the base and no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability was across southeast New Mexico as a result of intense surface heating. There was also a lingering outflow boundary from previous thunderstorms across the area. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that produced a funnel cloud in southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud/landspout about seven miles north of Artesia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest mid 40s to mid 50s dew points were located across southern New Mexico with a weak disturbance moving through northerly flow aloft.  Temperatures during the day warmed to near 100 degrees over the lowlands resulting in a high temperature-dew point spread which contributed to severe wind gusts to 74 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded at the San Augustin Pass Mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of November 2020. November saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 79 percent of the state. While a couple storm systems impacted the area throughout the month, only relatively light precipitation was recorded with each. Western New Mexico generally saw precipitation totals around half an inch to an inch while central and eastern New Mexico received amounts less than half an inch. The northern high terrain continued to fare the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from October 2020 continued in November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level trough moved into the Northern Rockies and plains states. Strong winds on the southern periphery of the trough affected the higher elevations of the Guadalupe Mountains in New Mexico and Texas.
A deep upper level trough dug into the Four Corners Region then moved into the South Plains. Strong winds associated with the trough affected the Guadalupe Mountains.

Upper ridge across the western US provided a NW flow regime into the the forecast area. Complex dropping over the eastern extension of the ridge out of NM provided enough surface forcing along cold pools centered over the eastern NM plains. Strong wind gusts were the primary concern with high downdraft CAPE environment available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm embedded within a line of storms moving from NW to SE across SE New Mexico provided a severe wind gust at the Tatum mesonet in northern Lea county NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front moved quickly south down the Front Range of the Rockies and into northeast New Mexico on November 29, 2020. A strong pressure gradient associated with the cold front and downslope flow into the Raton area produced a brief high wind gust up to 59 mph. North winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph were common across the remainder of northeast and east-central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief wind gust up to 59 mph was reported at the Raton airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large-scale upper level trough taking shape over the western United States forced two storm systems across New Mexico on November 7-9, 2020. Southwest winds spread across the state on November 7th then increased dramatically overnight with gusts of 60 to 70 mph reported in the high terrain through November 8th. Valley rain showers, a few thunderstorms, and mountain snow impacted much of northern and central New Mexico with the first storm system. Colder air filtered into the state with the second system on November 9th and led to more rain and mountain snow. Strong winds continued to impact areas along the central mountain chain and over parts of eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moving through Taos produced a brief wind gust up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large-scale upper level trough taking shape over the western United States forced two storm systems across New Mexico on November 7-9, 2020. Southwest winds spread across the state on November 7th then increased dramatically overnight with gusts of 60 to 70 mph reported in the high terrain through November 8th. Valley rain showers, a few thunderstorms, and mountain snow impacted much of northern and central New Mexico with the first storm system. Colder air filtered into the state with the second system on November 9th and led to more rain and mountain snow. Strong winds continued to impact areas along the central mountain chain and over parts of eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility site at Transwestern Pump reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 66 mph periodically for an extended period of time.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving upper level storm system approaching from the Great Basin with deepening surface low pressure over eastern New Mexico produced very strong west winds across New Mexico on November 14, 2020. Winds increased quickly after sunset then ramped up with gusts of 45 to 55 mph across much of the state. The strongest winds impacted areas along and east of the central mountain chain with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph and gusts of 60 to 70 mph. Blowing dust was reported with these strong winds as drought conditions continued to deteriorate across eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Angel Fire airport reported peak wind gusts up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest low level moisture and good mid level moisture combined with an area of surface convergence to produce significant rainfall across central Sierra County.  Weak upper level flow kept storm motion very slow which allowed for over 4 inches of rain to fall in an hour across Truth or Consequences and Williamsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was extensive flash flooding of roads and buildings around Truth or Consequences and Williamsburg. Near-zero visibility was reported in the heavy rain along with pea sized hail. A trained spotter reported 4.10 inches of rain in about one hour. A car was washed off the road.  Multiple homes were evacuated around the two towns.  The Rio Grande was threatening to exit its banks, therefore causing Elephant Butte Dam having to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an area of high pressure centered near the Four Corners, drier air began to move into western New Mexico. Meanwhile, an upper-level trough that moved through northeastern New Mexico provided the lift necessary for a round of afternoon thunderstorms. As the thunderstorms moved southeast off the higher terrain, they increased in coverage and intensity. A few severe thunderstorms were observed in southeastern New Mexico where strong winds and thunderstorm wind damage were reported. The drier mid-level air that moved into western portions of the state was responsible for virga and gusty winds, and the Albuquerque Sunport recorded a 61 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike killed two cows at the Pedernal COOP ranch. Damage amounts are estimated for the loss of cattle property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of August. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 95 percent of the state of which about 22 percent was deemed as extreme drought. A well below normal monsoon season continued across the state as an area of high pressure strengthened over the desert southwest. This resulted to record heat and dry conditions continuing throughout the month. Precipitation totals during the month of August were generally less than half an inch across western New Mexico with totals ranging around half an inch to near an inch in central New Mexico. Northeast New Mexico fared a little better with rain totals of about one to two inches, but this was still below normal for the area. August 2020 was one of the warmest and driest on record for New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from July 2020 continued in August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across northern portions of the Sandia/Manzano Mountains during July 2020. Below normal precipitation amounts set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure continued moving west into Arizona by the end of July while moisture was recharged across the state behind a strong back door cold front. North to south steering flow with abundant shear and instability led to the development of numerous showers and storms during the early to mid afternoon hours. Several of these storms were strong to severe as they progressed southward across the eastern plains and through the Rio Grande Valley. A storm near Las Vegas dumped hail up to the size of golf balls with very heavy rainfall and strong winds. Another storm moved south across Interstate 25 near San Jose and produced quarter size hail. Storms that developed over the Jemez Mountains moved south through Cochiti Pueblo with quarter size hail before blasting through the Albuquerque metro area with damaging winds and heavy rainfall. Ruidoso also experienced severe weather with quarter size hail and heavy rainfall. Minor flooding was reported in many areas as well. Swift runoff into the urban drainage system in Albuquerque washed two individuals down the Embudo Arroyo. One individual was rescued however the other died in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter north of Edgewood reported downed tree branches across State Road 344 along with minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep tap of monsoon moisture continued surging north into New Mexico on July 24, 2020, setting the stage for additional showers and thunderstorms with torrential rainfall, localized flash flooding, and strong microburst winds. Storms developed early in the afternoon then moved slowly north to northwest across most of New Mexico. Several of these thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall amounts over two inches along with small hail and strong microburst winds. The Clayton airport reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph and the Roswell airport reported a peak wind gust up to 68 mph. Straight-line winds damaged power lines, power poles, roofs, fences, and trees in the area around Midway. Heavy rainfall around Alamo damaged culverts and roadways in the area. A flash flood near Lemitar washed a pickup truck into an arroyo and caused damage to roadways. Fortunately, no injuries occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pickup truck crossing flood waters along CR-91 between Lemitar and Escondida was washed downstream. Occupants were able to safely abandon the vehicle before it was destroyed. Extent of injuries to the occupants of the vehicle are unknown. Damage amounts are estimated for loss of the truck.

With low-levels of the atmosphere remaining dry, the showers and thunderstorms that developed were mainly dry. A dry microburst near Bloomfield in San Juan County was responsible for uprooting trees and turning over a shed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst south of Bloomfield resulted in damage to a residence. Two trees were uprooted, a shed was overturned, and two house windows were broken. No injuries were reported and damage amounts are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The richest plume of monsoon moisture of the entire season set up over New Mexico on the weekend of July 25-26, 2020. The center of upper level high pressure drifted west into the state from west Texas while a weak upper level disturbance moved slowly north from eastern Arizona. The combination of several days of heavy rainfall with widespread showers and thunderstorms set the stage for flash flooding across the region. Rainfall amounts over the 48-hour period averaged three to five inches in the area from Socorro County north and east across the Manzano Mountains into the high plains of eastern New Mexico. The remainder of the state also picked up heavy rainfall averaging one to three inches from the Continental Divide eastward across the Rio Grande Valley into the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and northeast New Mexico. A quick burst of torrential rainfall around Madrid flooded several arroyos and damaged a couple roadways. Flash flooding occurred along parts of State Road 1 near Luis Lopez and washed one truck into the water. Another flash flood impacted the area around San Antonio resulting into the closure of U.S. Highway 380. Arroyos overflowed their banks between Bloomfield and Aztec, resulting in a few road closures and some minor damage to a couple homes. Minor flooding occurred in many other parts of the state including Aztec, Edgewood, Albuquerque, Gallup, and Dulce. Fortunately, there were no injuries from these heavy thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arroyos overflowed their banks along U.S. 550 between Aztec and Bloomfield. The roadway was closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure moved into southeastern New Mexico, a weak disturbance moved across the central Rockies. This disturbance combined with a backdoor cold front provided the necessary ingredients for isolated to scattered thunderstorms across portions of central and eastern New Mexico on August 28, 2020. Thunderstorms began to form early in the afternoon across the northern high terrain before moving east onto lower elevations. Strong downburst winds and gusty outflow boundaries were the biggest threats with these thunderstorms. The Raton airport recorded a 62 mph wind gust while an outflow boundary was responsible for a 60 mph gust in Rio Rancho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was estimated at the intersection of Unser Boulevard and Abrazo Road from a thunderstorm outflow boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico late August 28, 2020, replenished low-level moisture across the plains. A weak upper-level trough that was moving through southern Colorado and northeastern New Mexico provided additional lift and wind shear to produce isolated severe thunderstorms in the area. Upslope flow allowed for thunderstorms to form early in the day across the northern mountains before moving east into northeastern and east central portions of the state. The Raton airport recorded a 78 mph wind gust from a strong downburst. Strong winds also caused damage east of Abbott. Meanwhile, heavy rain from a severe thunderstorm washed out a bridge along Highway 456 in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved eastward along Highway 56 west of Abbott produced wind damage in the area. A COOP observer reported that a roof and two garage doors were blown off an outbuilding on a farm about twelve miles west of Abbott. Damage amounts are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the zone started the month in Severe(D2) drought and by the end of the month, the southern half of the zone was placed in Extreme(D3) drought. The Cooperative Observer in Redrock reported only 0.37 inches of rain in August which was 2.14 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with the northwest half of the zone in Severe(D2) drought, by the end of August, the western half of the zone was in Extreme(D3) drought with much of the eastern half in Severe(D2) drought. The month of August brought only 0.02 inches of rain to the Deming Airport which was 2.27 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwestern portion of the zone started the month in Severe(D2) drought and by the end of August, the southern half was in Extreme(D3) drought and the northern half in Severe(D2) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure continued moving west into Arizona by the end of July while moisture was recharged across the state behind a strong back door cold front. North to south steering flow with abundant shear and instability led to the development of numerous showers and storms during the early to mid afternoon hours. Several of these storms were strong to severe as they progressed southward across the eastern plains and through the Rio Grande Valley. A storm near Las Vegas dumped hail up to the size of golf balls with very heavy rainfall and strong winds. Another storm moved south across Interstate 25 near San Jose and produced quarter size hail. Storms that developed over the Jemez Mountains moved south through Cochiti Pueblo with quarter size hail before blasting through the Albuquerque metro area with damaging winds and heavy rainfall. Ruidoso also experienced severe weather with quarter size hail and heavy rainfall. Minor flooding was reported in many areas as well. Swift runoff into the urban drainage system in Albuquerque washed two individuals down the Embudo Arroyo. One individual was rescued however the other died in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 mph wind gust was recorded at the Albuquerque Sunport as a severe thunderstorm tracked south through the metro area. Blowing dust and reduced visibility were also observed with the strong winds. Numerous downed trees and branches were reported across the east side of the city. Damage amounts are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slightly drier air moved east into New Mexico as high pressure strengthened over Arizona on August 1, 2020. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of northern New Mexico then moved south into nearby highlands and valleys of central New Mexico. The strongest cell moved into Torrance County where a peak wind gust up to 61 mph was observed at Clines Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported a peak wind gust up to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Return flow into New Mexico ushered in deeper moisture across the state on August 2, 2020. Showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain during the early afternoon hours then moved south with upper level high pressure centered over Arizona. The combination of northerly steering flow with strong shear, instability, and deep moisture allowed a few of these storms to become severe across central and eastern New Mexico through the early evening. Hail, severe microburst winds, heavy rainfall, and frequent lightning occurred with the strongest storms. Socorro and Roswell both reported peak wind gusts near 60 mph. A lightning strike destroyed an abandoned mobile home northeast of Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured in Socorro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far southwestern portion of the zone started August in Severe(D2) drought and by the end of the month, the far south was in Extreme(D3) drought. The remainder of the southern half was in Severe(D2) by the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 4th, the western part of the zone was placed in Severe(D2) drought and then Extreme(D3) by the end of the month.  The remainder of the zone was placed in Severe(D2) drought on August 25th. The Cooperative Observer in Buckhorn reported 1.50 inches of rain which was 1.38 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little rain through the first two-thirds of the monsoon season lead to drought conditions becoming worse across most of southern New Mexico. Some areas over southwest New Mexico were placed into Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of high pressure over southern New Mexico allowed showers and thunderstorms to fire up along the Continental Divide during the afternoon then drift east into the Rio Grande Valley through the early evening hours. The airmass in place over the region was rather dry for mid August and many storms produced strong, erratic wind gusts. A brief storm that drifted into the Albuquerque metro area produced a brief wind gust up to 58 mph along with blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief thunderstorm at the Albuquerque Sunport produced a downburst wind gust up to 58 mph along with blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and unstable airmass with sufficient mid level moisture over central New Mexico set the stage for isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms on August 15, 2020. A brief landspout tornado was observed beneath a developing thunderstorm over far northwest Albuquerque. This brief landspout was seen mostly as a very large dust swirl for a couple minutes. No damage occurred with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado was observed over far northwest Albuquerque on a rural mesa. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance in northerly flow aloft combined with a dry low level atmosphere to produce a couple of severe wind reports in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico DOT site located west of Lordsburg. Some blowing dust was also reported in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance in northerly flow aloft combined with a dry low level atmosphere to produce a couple of severe wind reports in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm moved south across the White Sands Missile Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture streaming into eastern New Mexico from the Gulf Coast and northern Mexico interacted with a weak shortwave trough approaching from the west to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms over the region on July 4, 2020. Showers and storms first developed along the central mountain chain then spread southeast into the eastern plains where very heavy rainfall, strong wind gusts, hail, and frequent lightning impacted the region. A series of slow-moving storms developed over the Pine Lodge burn scar in Lincoln County and dumped an estimated one to two inches of rain over the area. Flash flooding was observed along NM-246 between mile markers 30 and 31 with up to 12 inches of water, mud, and debris over the roadway across a 30 yard stretch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down over rural countryside 12 miles west of Grenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico late August 28, 2020, replenished low-level moisture across the plains. A weak upper-level trough that was moving through southern Colorado and northeastern New Mexico provided additional lift and wind shear to produce isolated severe thunderstorms in the area. Upslope flow allowed for thunderstorms to form early in the day across the northern mountains before moving east into northeastern and east central portions of the state. The Raton airport recorded a 78 mph wind gust from a strong downburst. Strong winds also caused damage east of Abbott. Meanwhile, heavy rain from a severe thunderstorm washed out a bridge along Highway 456 in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported one inch hail from a severe thunderstorm west of Santa Fe. The observer also reported damaged shingles from the hail. Damage amounts are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of August. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 95 percent of the state of which about 22 percent was deemed as extreme drought. A well below normal monsoon season continued across the state as an area of high pressure strengthened over the desert southwest. This resulted to record heat and dry conditions continuing throughout the month. Precipitation totals during the month of August were generally less than half an inch across western New Mexico with totals ranging around half an inch to near an inch in central New Mexico. Northeast New Mexico fared a little better with rain totals of about one to two inches, but this was still below normal for the area. August 2020 was one of the warmest and driest on record for New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from July 2020 continued in August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The richest plume of monsoon moisture of the entire season set up over New Mexico on the weekend of July 25-26, 2020. The center of upper level high pressure drifted west into the state from west Texas while a weak upper level disturbance moved slowly north from eastern Arizona. The combination of several days of heavy rainfall with widespread showers and thunderstorms set the stage for flash flooding across the region. Rainfall amounts over the 48-hour period averaged three to five inches in the area from Socorro County north and east across the Manzano Mountains into the high plains of eastern New Mexico. The remainder of the state also picked up heavy rainfall averaging one to three inches from the Continental Divide eastward across the Rio Grande Valley into the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and northeast New Mexico. A quick burst of torrential rainfall around Madrid flooded several arroyos and damaged a couple roadways. Flash flooding occurred along parts of State Road 1 near Luis Lopez and washed one truck into the water. Another flash flood impacted the area around San Antonio resulting into the closure of U.S. Highway 380. Arroyos overflowed their banks between Bloomfield and Aztec, resulting in a few road closures and some minor damage to a couple homes. Minor flooding occurred in many other parts of the state including Aztec, Edgewood, Albuquerque, Gallup, and Dulce. Fortunately, there were no injuries from these heavy thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management and NMDOT officials reported flash flooding in the area between Luis Lopez and San Antonio. State Road 1 was closed between mile markers 51 and 60 due to flood waters and debris over the roadway. One vehicle was trapped in flood waters up to the mirrors near U.S. Highway 380 around San Antonio. U.S. Highway 380 was closed east of San Antonio. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for repairs to several roadways, culverts, low water crossings, and the flooded vehicle. Extent of injuries to the occupants of the flooded vehicle are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across central Torrance County during July 2020. Below normal precipitation amounts set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

Arroyos overflowed their banks between Bloomfield and Aztec, resulting in a few road closures and some minor damage to a couple homes. Minor flooding occurred in many other parts of the state including Aztec, Edgewood, Albuquerque, Gallup, and Dulce. Fortunately, there were no injuries from these heavy thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sudden torrent of rainfall filled several arroyos around Madrid and Cerrillos. Flood damage was reported to low water crossings, culverts, and driveways. Damage amounts are a very rough estimate for repairs to several roadways and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent pattern with northwest flow aloft combined with return flow across eastern New Mexico continued on August 3, 2020. With deep shear, instability, and moisture still in place across the area, another day with isolated severe thunderstorms occurred. The strongest storms occurred across the northeastern corner of the state where hail, strong downburst winds, frequent lightning, and heavy downpours were observed. Two inch hail was reported near Buena Vista. A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was recorded in Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Raton airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of August. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 95 percent of the state of which about 22 percent was deemed as extreme drought. A well below normal monsoon season continued across the state as an area of high pressure strengthened over the desert southwest. This resulted to record heat and dry conditions continuing throughout the month. Precipitation totals during the month of August were generally less than half an inch across western New Mexico with totals ranging around half an inch to near an inch in central New Mexico. Northeast New Mexico fared a little better with rain totals of about one to two inches, but this was still below normal for the area. August 2020 was one of the warmest and driest on record for New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from July 2020 continued in August.
