EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved into eastern New Mexico recharged moisture over the region on August 5, 2020. Thunderstorm coverage increased over the area however most activity was light across the western half of the state. Stronger storms with heavier rainfall amounts were able to impact eastern New Mexico. A nearly stationary storm around Bonito Lake caused flash flooding on the Little Bear burn scar. Radar estimated between one and two inches of rain fell which washed out old debris in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm over the Little Bear burn scar produced flash flooding near Bonito Lake. Rocks, mud, and debris that had built up in the area was washed out onto nearby roads. Damage amounts are estimated for culverts and one roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry westerly flow over a majority of the state led to a significant decrease in storm coverage on August 9, 2020. Enough low-level moisture was still in place, however, across eastern New Mexico which allowed for another round of daytime thunderstorms. A brief thunderstorm near Clovis produced a peak wind gust up to 59 mph. Heavy rain was also observed with 1.95 inches of rain recorded at Cannon Air Force Base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph was measured at Cannon Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of July. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 94 percent of the state of which about 14 percent across northern and southeast New Mexico was deemed as extreme drought. Monsoon season got off to a slow start during the month of July as an area of high pressure strengthened over the state. Record heat and dry conditions were observed across the area for several days. A more traditional monsoon pattern developed toward mid-July with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall. Northeast New Mexico received above normal rainfall totals during the latter half of the month, and Clayton recorded their 6th wettest July on record with 5.84 of rainfall. Despite the more active pattern, storm tracks did not favor northwest and southeast New Mexico where below normal precipitation totals and worsening drought conditions were observed. Precipitation totals during the month of July generally ranged from two to three inches across southwest and south central New Mexico with one to three inches across central New Mexico and the Rio Grande Valley. Northeast New Mexico received the most amount of rainfall with CoCoRaHS observers in Union County receiving about five to seven inches. Southeast New Mexico and the Four Corners area received the least amount of precipitation with rain totals ranging from one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from June 2020 continued in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure continued moving west into Arizona by the end of July while moisture was recharged across the state behind a strong back door cold front. North to south steering flow with abundant shear and instability led to the development of numerous showers and storms during the early to mid afternoon hours. Several of these storms were strong to severe as they progressed southward across the eastern plains and through the Rio Grande Valley. A storm near Las Vegas dumped hail up to the size of golf balls with very heavy rainfall and strong winds. Another storm moved south across Interstate 25 near San Jose and produced quarter size hail. Storms that developed over the Jemez Mountains moved south through Cochiti Pueblo with quarter size hail before blasting through the Albuquerque metro area with damaging winds and heavy rainfall. Ruidoso also experienced severe weather with quarter size hail and heavy rainfall. Minor flooding was reported in many areas as well. Swift runoff into the urban drainage system in Albuquerque washed two individuals down the Embudo Arroyo. One individual was rescued however the other died in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various sources reported hail up to the size of quarters with a severe thunderstorm that tracked south through Alto and Ruidoso.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men occupying the Embudo Arroyo were swept away from flood waters that occurred with a severe thunderstorm over the Albuquerque east side. One man was rescued with help from bystanders. The other man was unable to be rescued and was found dead in the urban catchment pond downstream later in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of September 2020. As one of the worst monsoon seasons on record came to end, moderate to extreme drought worsened to nearly 100 percent of the state of which 40 percent was deemed as extreme to exceptional drought. An anomalous early season winter storm brought high terrain snow and rain to most of the area during the first week of September. Precipitation totals across central and western New Mexico generally ranged from half an inch to three quarters an inch while precipitation totals neared an inch across north central and northeast New Mexico. Portions of the higher terrain of south central New Mexico fared better with totals generally near an inch and a half. Despite this, the second half of the month was mostly dry with more record heat which contributed to the worsening drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from August 2020 continued in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking heatwave during the first week of September took a dramatic turn toward winter as a powerful storm system moved south through the Rocky Mountains on September 8, 2020. A powerful cold front shifted south and west through eastern New Mexico and plowed through gaps in the central mountain chain into the Rio Grande Valley on the morning of September 9, 2020. Canyon winds peaked during the late afternoon and evening hours when gusts of 70 to 80 mph were reported. These powerful winds affected the east side of the Albuquerque metro area for several hours before slowly tapering off through September 10, 2020. Dozens of trees, power lines, power poles, fences, and walls were blown down. Roof damage was also reported across the city. At least 15,000 customers were without power, and as many as a 1,000 customers had no power for three days after the event. Record breaking early snowfall also impacted the high terrain with this event. Many locations in the northern mountains reported two to six inches of snow while one to two inches fell at lower elevations of northern New Mexico. Fire and rescue personnel evacuated the Rio Chama RV Park as the combination of heavy snow and strong winds downed several large tree limbs onto at least two recreational vehicles. Several locations also set daily record low minimum and record low maximum temperatures on both September 9th and September 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust up to 89 mph was measured at the top of the Sandia Peak Tramway. The tram was closed for the day due to high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Sunport measured damaging wind gusts between 58 mph and 71 mph for several hours as gap winds plowed through Tijeras Canyon. Dozens of large trees were toppled across the city, including; one tree along Girard just south of Gibson, one tree along Sunport Loop near the Sheraton hotel, at least one tree on the University of New Mexico campus, and other large trees at Roosevelt Park and Hyder Park. A large pine tree at Jefferson Middle School fell over power lines along Girard Blvd. Another large pine tree fell over power lines into Girard near Campus Blvd. Roofing was damaged at the city courthouse in downtown Albuquerque and another roof was damaged at a hotel near the Sunport. Thousands of PNM customers were without power for one to two days and some others were without power for three to four days. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for damage to power infrastructure, roofs, and fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was located over northern Chihuahua with a weak convergence area extending north through the Rio Grande Valley.  This convergent area combined with a disturbance moving through a northeast flow aloft to produce a severe thunderstorm with a wind gust to 83 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power poles down in northeast Las Cruces.  At 8 am the following day, 500 customers were still without electricity. Mesonet site which recorded the gust was located north of Highway 70 and east of I-25. Pea size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of September 2020. As one of the worst monsoon seasons on record came to end, moderate to extreme drought worsened to nearly 100 percent of the state of which 40 percent was deemed as extreme to exceptional drought. An anomalous early season winter storm brought high terrain snow and rain to most of the area during the first week of September. Precipitation totals across central and western New Mexico generally ranged from half an inch to three quarters an inch while precipitation totals neared an inch across north central and northeast New Mexico. Portions of the higher terrain of south central New Mexico fared better with totals generally near an inch and a half. Despite this, the second half of the month was mostly dry with more record heat which contributed to the worsening drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from August 2020 continued in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving quickly southeast through the southern Rockies forced a strong cold front through New Mexico on September 1, 2020. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the frontal boundary by the mid morning hours across western New Mexico before racing southeast through the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. Strong forcing along the cold front combined with sufficient moisture and instability over the area to produce a few severe thunderstorms with large hail, high winds, and heavy rainfall. Several locations reported wind gusts near 60 mph, including Clines Corners, Fort Sumner, and Roswell. This cold front ushered in the coolest air since early June for New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported near Tome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record breaking heatwave during the first week of September took a dramatic turn toward winter as a powerful storm system moved south through the Rocky Mountains on September 8, 2020. A powerful cold front shifted south and west through eastern New Mexico and plowed through gaps in the central mountain chain into the Rio Grande Valley on the morning of September 9, 2020. Canyon winds peaked during the late afternoon and evening hours when gusts of 70 to 80 mph were reported. These powerful winds affected the east side of the Albuquerque metro area for several hours before slowly tapering off through September 10, 2020. Dozens of trees, power lines, power poles, fences, and walls were blown down. Roof damage was also reported across the city. At least 15,000 customers were without power, and as many as a 1,000 customers had no power for three days after the event. Record breaking early snowfall also impacted the high terrain with this event. Many locations in the northern mountains reported two to six inches of snow while one to two inches fell at lower elevations of northern New Mexico. Fire and rescue personnel evacuated the Rio Chama RV Park as the combination of heavy snow and strong winds downed several large tree limbs onto at least two recreational vehicles. Several locations also set daily record low minimum and record low maximum temperatures on both September 9th and September 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 43 mph periodically for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of September 2020. As one of the worst monsoon seasons on record came to end, moderate to extreme drought worsened to nearly 100 percent of the state of which 40 percent was deemed as extreme to exceptional drought. An anomalous early season winter storm brought high terrain snow and rain to most of the area during the first week of September. Precipitation totals across central and western New Mexico generally ranged from half an inch to three quarters an inch while precipitation totals neared an inch across north central and northeast New Mexico. Portions of the higher terrain of south central New Mexico fared better with totals generally near an inch and a half. Despite this, the second half of the month was mostly dry with more record heat which contributed to the worsening drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from August 2020 continued in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As monsoonal moisture continued to stream into New Mexico, a backdoor cold front that moved through eastern NM helped set the stage for severe weather on July 15, 2020. With increasing low-level moisture, instability, and shear focused along this boundary, the storms that moved off the central mountain chain interacted with the boundary and intensified. Through the afternoon and evening hours, the storms grew upscale into a broken line that moved northeast through the eastern plains. Strong thunderstorm winds were reported ahead of these storms while large hail was reported near Capulin. A brief gustnado was also observed in rural Socorro County along a strong outflow boundary moving through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving outflow boundary helped develop a dusty landspout tornado over rural Socorro County a few miles east of Veguita. No damage occurred as a result of this weak tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very dry month with little in the way of monsoon moisture worsened drought conditions across southwest and south-central New Mexico. Some areas around the Gila region were placed into Exceptional(D4) drought conditions while most other areas were at least in Severe(D2) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Severe(D2) drought over the west and spread to all areas but the northeast by the end of the month. The Cooperative Observer at New Mexico State University reported 0.43 inches of precipitation which was 0.90 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure system responsible for several consecutive days of record high temperatures and dry conditions finally began to weaken and shift eastward toward the Texas Panhandle. This allowed mid-level moisture to stream into portions of western and central NM which resulted in scattered thunderstorms developing during the afternoon and evening hours on July 13, 2020. Storms initially formed over the western and northern high terrain early in the afternoon before moving eastward into valley locations. With drier low-levels, the main hazards with these storms was strong outflow winds. One severe storm in the Albuquerque metro area produced winds near 60 mph which reduced visibility and downed a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was reported at Clines Corners from the same thunderstorm that produced damaging winds in Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of December 2020. December once again saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 82 percent of the state. While the overall pattern remained active with a few storm systems impacting the state, a lack of deep moisture generally resulted in only light precipitation. The exception was across the northern high terrain which benefited from favorable storm tracks and a couple heavy snow events. Most of the state generally saw precipitation totals around a quarter to a half an inch. The northern high terrain continued to fare the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions from November 2020 continued in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low moved into northern New Mexico. Strong winds on the south side of this system mixed into the higher elevations of the Guadalupe Mountains.
An upper level storm system moved across the northern tier of states. Winds in the Guadalupe Mountains increased and briefly became strong as the trough moved across the area.

Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of December 2020. December once again saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 82 percent of the state. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system impacted northern New Mexico with heavy mountain snow on December 28-29, 2020. Snow was largely confined to the northern high terrain which picked up several inches of snow. The Tusas and northern Sangre de Cristo mountains picked up about 10 to 12 inches of snow. Additionally, a brief period of freezing rain was observed across the northeast during the morning hours of December 29th. This led to localized icy and slick spots along portions of U.S. Highway 64 between Clayton and Raton as well as Interstate 25 near Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various locations reported 8 to 10 inches of snow in the area. A CoCoRaHS observer northeast of Ensenada measured 9.5 inches of snow on the morning of December 29th while the Chamita SNOTEL had an estimated 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system impacted northern New Mexico with heavy mountain snow on December 28-29, 2020. Snow was largely confined to the northern high terrain which picked up several inches of snow. The Tusas and northern Sangre de Cristo mountains picked up about 10 to 12 inches of snow. Additionally, a brief period of freezing rain was observed across the northeast during the morning hours of December 29th. This led to localized icy and slick spots along portions of U.S. Highway 64 between Clayton and Raton as well as Interstate 25 near Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various SNOTEL locations in the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed heavy snow with total accumulations of about 8 to 13 inches. Ski sites in the Red River area also saw heavy accumulations with estimates over one foot.

While the overall pattern remained active with a few storm systems impacting the state, a lack of deep moisture generally resulted in only light precipitation. The exception was across the northern high terrain which benefited from favorable storm tracks and a couple heavy snow events. Most of the state generally saw precipitation totals around a quarter to a half an inch. The northern high terrain continued to fare the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from November 2020 continued in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large-scale upper level trough taking shape over the western United States forced two storm systems across New Mexico on November 7-9, 2020. Southwest winds spread across the state on November 7th then increased dramatically overnight with gusts of 60 to 70 mph reported in the high terrain through November 8th. Valley rain showers, a few thunderstorms, and mountain snow impacted much of northern and central New Mexico with the first storm system. Colder air filtered into the state with the second system on November 9th and led to more rain and mountain snow. Strong winds continued to impact areas along the central mountain chain and over parts of eastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various sites across the Sacramento Mountains, including Sierra Blanca Regional airport, reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 66 mph periodically over an extended period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow in the wake of a departing storm system provided typically windy conditions at Clines Corners on November 25, 2020. A brief wind gust up to 58 mph was reported during the late morning hours. Despite strong winds at Clines Corners the remainder of the state was generally tranquil with wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief wind gust up to 58 mph occurred at Clines Corners.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of October 2020. October saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 67 percent of the state. Only one system impacted the state during the month which was a major, historic winter storm that brought widespread snow and record cold to the area. Western New Mexico generally saw precipitation totals around half an inch to an inch while central and eastern New Mexico received amounts around three-quarters of an inch to an inch. The northern high terrain fared the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches. Despite this welcome precipitation and snow pack, it had no improvement on drought conditions across the state thanks to persistent dry conditions that have been ongoing for many months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from September 2020 worsened to extreme drought across most of the area in October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front surged southwest through eastern New Mexico on October 25, 2020 in advance of a potent upper level storm system approaching from the west. A record cold airmass over eastern New Mexico shifted west through the central mountain chain while precipitation began developing northward over central and western New Mexico early on the 26th. Precipitation changed over to all snow and several heavy snow bands impacted northern and western NM through the 27th. Areas along and west of the central mountain chain picked up eight to 16 inches of snow with up to 22 inches in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The Albuquerque metro area even picked up between five and 10 inches of snow with well over 100 vehicle accidents reported. A second round of heavy snow developed along the central mountain chain into eastern New Mexico from late on the 27th into the 28th. Blizzard conditions were reported over parts of northeast NM with 12 to 20 inches of snow, strong north winds, drifts of one to four feet, severe travel conditions, and road closures. Warmer air began filtering into the area by late on the 28th resulting in a mixture of rain and snow. Dozens of record low maximum and record low minimum temperatures were broken. Record daily snowfall records were also set for the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around Elk and Dunken ranged from three to seven inches.

Warmer air began filtering into the area by late on the 28th resulting in a mixture of rain and snow. Dozens of record low maximum and record low minimum temperatures were broken. Record daily snowfall records were also set for the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various stations reported between six and 14 inches of snow in the area from Mora to Angel Fire and Eagle Nest. The 14 inches reported at Eagle Nest on October 26th set a new daily record and the storm total of 20 inches set a new monthly record snowfall for October. Records at Eagle Nest date back to 1929.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued through October which kept most areas in Severe(D2) to Exceptional(D4) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of October 2020. October saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 67 percent of the state. Only one system impacted the state during the month which was a major, historic winter storm that brought widespread snow and record cold to the area. Western New Mexico generally saw precipitation totals around half an inch to an inch while central and eastern New Mexico received amounts around three-quarters of an inch to an inch. The northern high terrain fared the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches. Despite this welcome precipitation and snow pack, it had no improvement on drought conditions across the state thanks to persistent dry conditions that have been ongoing for many months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from September 2020 continued in October. Exceptional drought conditions began to develop across western portions of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful jet stream moving through the central Rockies allowed strong mountain wave activity to develop along the east slopes of the central mountain chain on October 14, 2020. West to northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 40 mph were common in wind prone locations east of the higher terrain. The strongest winds impacted the area around Clines Corners where gusts up to 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 44 mph periodically for a few hours.

Snow accumulations of up to seven inches were recorded in the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gila Hot Springs recorded 4.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of October 2020. October saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 67 percent of the state. Only one system impacted the state during the month which was a major, historic winter storm that brought widespread snow and record cold to the area. Western New Mexico generally saw precipitation totals around half an inch to an inch while central and eastern New Mexico received amounts around three-quarters of an inch to an inch. The northern high terrain fared the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches. Despite this welcome precipitation and snow pack, it had no improvement on drought conditions across the state thanks to persistent dry conditions that have been ongoing for many months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across the area during October 2020. Below normal precipitation amounts set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front surged southwest through eastern New Mexico on October 25, 2020 in advance of a potent upper level storm system approaching from the west. A record cold airmass over eastern New Mexico shifted west through the central mountain chain while precipitation began developing northward over central and western New Mexico early on the 26th. Precipitation changed over to all snow and several heavy snow bands impacted northern and western NM through the 27th. Areas along and west of the central mountain chain picked up eight to 16 inches of snow with up to 22 inches in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The Albuquerque metro area even picked up between five and 10 inches of snow with well over 100 vehicle accidents reported. A second round of heavy snow developed along the central mountain chain into eastern New Mexico from late on the 27th into the 28th. Blizzard conditions were reported over parts of northeast NM with 12 to 20 inches of snow, strong north winds, drifts of one to four feet, severe travel conditions, and road closures. Warmer air began filtering into the area by late on the 28th resulting in a mixture of rain and snow. Dozens of record low maximum and record low minimum temperatures were broken. Record daily snowfall records were also set for the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from eight to 16 inches in the area from near Glorieta to Rowe.

Record daily snowfall records were also set for the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico continued to worsen during the month of October 2020. October saw below normal precipitation across the entire area with extreme to exceptional drought worsening to about 67 percent of the state. Only one system impacted the state during the month which was a major, historic winter storm that brought widespread snow and record cold to the area. Western New Mexico generally saw precipitation totals around half an inch to an inch while central and eastern New Mexico received amounts around three-quarters of an inch to an inch. The northern high terrain fared the best with liquid equivalent amounts of one to two inches. Despite this welcome precipitation and snow pack, it had no improvement on drought conditions across the state thanks to persistent dry conditions that have been ongoing for many months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from September 2020 continued in October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough moving through the central Rockies forced a powerful cold front through New Mexico on June 8, 2020. Strong west to southwest winds ahead of the front impacted much of central and western New Mexico with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. Winds then shifted to the west and northwest as the front plowed through the state with localized peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph. A few record low temperatures were reported in the wake of the front with lows in the 20s and 30s over some northern and western valleys on the morning of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton airpark reported powerful sustained winds between 40 mph and 53 mph for several hours. No damage was observed in Clayton although power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front that moved through the state the previous day replenished moisture throughout the area on June 23, 2020. With northwest flow aloft, deep shear and instability was in place throughout central and eastern NM. This resulted in widespread thunderstorms with several severe storms throughout the afternoon and evening. Storms initially formed early in the afternoon over the northern high terrain of New Mexico before moving east-southeast into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. The most intense thunderstorms were across the eastern plains where storms that produced large hail and damaging winds continued into the evening hours. A strong thunderstorm also affected the Albuquerque metro area, and penny sized hail fell at the Albuquerque WFO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary ahead of a severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 66 mph near White Sands Missile Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow aloft combined with moist southeasterly flow at the surface to produce favorable conditions for strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern and south central New Mexico on June 24, 2020. Isolated storms formed initially over the northern and western high terrain before moving east-southeast into lower elevations. Storms that initially formed over the western high terrain produced very strong downburst winds with the ASOS station at the Gallup airport recording a 62 mph wind gust. As the storms moved east-southeast into Socorro County, hail became the primary threat, and one strong thunderstorm produced penny size hail near San Acacia. A strong thunderstorm also formed over the Ute Park burn scar where it produced heavy rain and nickel size hail, but no flooding impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst produced a brief wind gust up to 62 mph at Gallup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent pattern over New Mexico with northwest flow aloft combined with moist southeasterly flow at the surface led to another day with isolated severe storms across eastern NM on June 25, 2020. Isolated storms formed initially over the northern high terrain of New Mexico before moving east-southeast onto the eastern plains where they intensified. As the storms moved into a more favorable environment, they became more organized and formed a small line that moved east-southeast roughly from Las Vegas to Clovis, New Mexico. This line of thunderstorms produced a very strong outflow boundary with gusts over 50 mph. Blowing dust was reported along and ahead of this outflow boundary with reduced visibility in portions of Quay and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Rosa AWOS measured a 61 mph wind gust as a broken line of thunderstorms moved southeast through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days with a severe thunderstorm risk across eastern New Mexico, an upper-level ridge began to build over the state on June 26, 2020 that lessened the severe risk. Low-level moisture began to decrease as drier southwesterly flow set up over the area. Still, enough moisture remained across northeast and east central New Mexico to allow for isolated storms to once again develop through the afternoon hours. With relatively drier low-levels in this area, these storms posed a greater wind threat with strong thunderstorm wind gusts being measured across portions of east central New Mexico. A strong outflow boundary associated with an isolated storm near Tucumcari produced a wind gust of 59 mph at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary associated with an isolated thunderstorm produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Tucumcari airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The New Mexico State Forestry Division reported that the Farm Camp Fire started on Saturday, June 6, 2020, about five miles west of Mosquero, New Mexico off of Highway 39 and Farm Camp Road in Harding County. The fire was caused by a lightning strike that occurred with thunderstorms earlier in the week, followed by a critical fire weather pattern by midweek. The fire grew rapidly to around 12,000 acres within 24 hours due to strong winds ahead of an approaching Pacific cold front. Evacuations were ordered in the area around Mosquero and one abandoned structure was burned. The fire was around 50% contained on June 10th then nearly 100% contained by June 14th. The Farm Camp Fire burned a total of 22,872 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One abandoned home was destroyed by the Farm Camp Fire near Mosquero. The condition of the structure before the fire and value are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shallow moisture along a weak boundary over eastern New Mexico interacted with just enough instability beneath northwest flow aloft to produce isolated thunderstorms over the Caprock region on June 27, 2020. One storm was able to strengthen enough in the Clovis area to produce a brief wind gust up to 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis airport reported a peak wind gust up to 63 mph as a thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep tap of moisture surged north into New Mexico ahead of a strong Pacific cold front approaching from the west on June 5-6, 2020. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over western New Mexico during the afternoon hours of June 5th before spreading slowly east into the Rio Grande Valley through the morning of the June 6th. Strong south to southwest flow ahead of the front combined with showers and thunderstorms to produce localized high winds over parts of the area. Significantly drier and cooler conditions moved into the area in the wake of this front on strong west to northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NM Air Quality mesonet site northeast of Farmington reported a thunderstorm wind gust up to 58 mph. A large Cottonwood tree at a residence in Farmington was also partially blown down from high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of June. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 53 percent of the state of which about 11 percent across northern NM was deemed as extreme drought. Very dry conditions continued through the first half of June with a more active pattern presenting itself at the end of the month. Despite the more active pattern, storm coverage and precipitation totals were not enough to improve drought conditions especially across the north with the extreme drought conditions. Precipitation totals during the month of June ranged from 1 to 2 inches across central and eastern NM with less than an inch across north central and northeast NM. The Four Corners area of the state continued to receive the least amount of precipitation with rain totals less than half an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across Roosevelt County during June 2020. Below normal precipitation amounts set the stage for deteriorating drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across northern and eastern New Mexico continued to deteriorate during the month of June. Moderate to extreme drought worsened to about 53 percent of the state of which about 11 percent across northern NM was deemed as extreme drought. Very dry conditions continued through the first half of June with a more active pattern presenting itself at the end of the month. Despite the more active pattern, storm coverage and precipitation totals were not enough to improve drought conditions especially across the north with the extreme drought conditions. Precipitation totals during the month of June ranged from 1 to 2 inches across central and eastern NM with less than an inch across north central and northeast NM. The Four Corners area of the state continued to receive the least amount of precipitation with rain totals less than half an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from May 2020 worsened to extreme drought for a majority of Union County in June.

Significantly drier and cooler conditions moved into the area in the wake of this front on strong west to northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos airport reported sustained winds between 40 mph and 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front that moved through the state the previous day replenished moisture throughout the area on June 23, 2020. With northwest flow aloft, deep shear and instability was in place throughout central and eastern NM. This resulted in widespread thunderstorms with several severe storms throughout the afternoon and evening. Storms initially formed early in the afternoon over the northern high terrain of New Mexico before moving east-southeast into the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains. The most intense thunderstorms were across the eastern plains where storms that produced large hail and damaging winds continued into the evening hours. A strong thunderstorm also affected the Albuquerque metro area, and penny sized hail fell at the Albuquerque WFO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser near Milagro reported hail up to golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough moving through the central Rockies forced a powerful cold front through New Mexico on June 8, 2020. Strong west to southwest winds ahead of the front impacted much of central and western New Mexico with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. Winds then shifted to the west and northwest as the front plowed through the state with localized peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph. A few record low temperatures were reported in the wake of the front with lows in the 20s and 30s over some northern and western valleys on the morning of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front ripped through Farmington with peak wind gusts near 59 mph. Visibility was reduced to between two and three miles in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved into northeast and east central New Mexico on June 22, 2020. Instability and shear along this boundary combined with northwest flow aloft and deep moisture set the stage for severe weather across the eastern plains. Storms initially formed early in the afternoon over the northern high terrain of New Mexico before moving southeast onto the plains where they intensified through the evening hours. The most intense storm was near Portales where hail up to the size of tennis balls was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Portales reported large hail in the area ranging from half-dollar to tennis ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level storm system drifting south through the southern Rockies interacted with a back door cold front sliding west into central New Mexico to produce a narrow band of heavy snowfall during the early morning hours of December 2, 2020. Snow first developed over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains before pushing south and west into the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas where snow persisted for several hours. The heaviest snow was reported in a narrow band over southern Rio Rancho where four to eight inches fell. The remainder of central New Mexico picked up just a couple inches. The combination of very cold temperatures with snow resulted in significant travel impacts along Interstate 40 between Edgewood and Albuquerque. A major semi-truck accident took place near Sedillo Hill where 10 vehicles were damaged or destroyed. Remarkably, New Mexico State Police reported that only two drivers were transported to a local hospital with minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very slick travel conditions along Interstate 40 between Tijeras and Sedillo Hill resulted in multiple crashes involving 10 semi-trucks. Remarkably, only two motorists were sent to a local hospital for minor injuries. Damage amounts are a rough estimate for significant damage or total loss to several of the commercial vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal upper-level jet with wind speeds over 100 knots located near the New Mexico and Colorado border was responsible for mountain wave activity over the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the overnight hours on December 26 and December 27, 2020. Very strong downslope winds were recorded in the Raton Area with wind gusts over 70 mph observed for several hours at the airport. These strong winds were also responsible for a power outage at the Raton airport which lasted into the early afternoon hours of December 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton airport recorded several hours of high winds overnight. The highest sustained wind speeds were measured at 48 mph with the highest wind gust up to 72 mph.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving winter storm system impacted northern New Mexico with heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, and strong winds on December 12-13, 2020. This storm system produced heavy snowfall in a short period of time across the northern high terrain and parts of northeast New Mexico near the Colorado border. Snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour led to low visibility and significant accumulations in around 12 hours. Much of the northern mountains picked up eight to 14 inches with localized amounts as a high as 20 inches. Very cold temperatures and strong winds over northeast New Mexico led to the closure of U.S. Highway 64 between Raton and Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy snowfall, strong winds, blowing snow, and low visibility resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64 between Raton, Capulin, and Clayton. Snowfall amounts averaged 3 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low dug into the southwestern U.S. and northern Mexico. Cold air moving into the region ahead of the low caused rain showers to turn to snow and snow showers. Mostly light amounts of snow fell across southeastern New Mexico with one isolated heavy event in Queen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated four inches of snow fell in Queen.
